title: Listening - Part 3: Changing Daily Habits
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a change they have made to their daily routine. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives for making the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a change they have made to their daily routine. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the reason each speaker gives for making the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about changing one of their daily habits.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I used to get into bed and then spend another hour checking messages and watching short videos. I'd feel tired the next morning, but still repeat the same pattern. A friend challenged me to leave my phone downstairs overnight. [19]Since I stopped looking at a bright screen just before bed, I fall asleep far more easily and wake up feeling properly rested.[/19] I sometimes miss late messages, but nobody has complained, and I wouldn't return to my old habit.||Speaker 2 Man: Until recently I bought lunch near the office every day. It was convenient, and there were plenty of cafés, so I didn't think much about it. Then I examined my bank account before a holiday and was shocked. [20]I now prepare sandwiches or salads at home because I realised how much I could keep each month by not paying for lunch out.[/20] It requires a little planning, but I can afford to do more interesting things at weekends.||Speaker 3 Woman: My first lecture starts at nine, and I used to throw my bag together in the morning. Inevitably I couldn't find my notes or keys, then I'd miss the bus. My tutor warned me about my attendance. [21]Now I pack everything and choose my clothes the night before, so I can leave immediately and get to campus on time.[/21] It's a tiny change, yet it has removed the most stressful part of my day.||Speaker 4 Man: I have an office job and could easily sit at a desk for eight hours without moving. When I had a routine appointment, the doctor said my blood pressure was beginning to rise and asked how active I was. [22]On her recommendation, I started taking a brisk walk during my lunch break rather than staying at my computer.[/22] At first it seemed like an interruption, but now I return to work with more energy as well.||Speaker 5 Woman: When I'm writing essays, I used to keep every notification switched on in case friends were arranging something. Of course, every message led to a conversation, and an hour would disappear. [23]I've started putting my phone in another room while I study because, without constant interruptions, I can actually focus on what I'm reading and finish much sooner.[/23] I catch up with friends afterwards, so I don't feel I'm missing out.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I used to get into bed and then spend another hour checking messages and watching short videos. I'd feel tired the next morning, but still repeat the same pattern. A friend challenged me to leave my phone downstairs overnight. Since I stopped looking at a bright screen just before bed, I fall asleep far more easily and wake up feeling properly rested. I sometimes miss late messages, but nobody has complained, and I wouldn't return to my old habit.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: Until recently I bought lunch near the office every day. It was convenient, and there were plenty of cafés, so I didn't think much about it. Then I examined my bank account before a holiday and was shocked. I now prepare sandwiches or salads at home because I realised how much I could keep each month by not paying for lunch out. It requires a little planning, but I can afford to do more interesting things at weekends.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My first lecture starts at nine, and I used to throw my bag together in the morning. Inevitably I couldn't find my notes or keys, then I'd miss the bus. My tutor warned me about my attendance. Now I pack everything and choose my clothes the night before, so I can leave immediately and get to campus on time. It's a tiny change, yet it has removed the most stressful part of my day.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I have an office job and could easily sit at a desk for eight hours without moving. When I had a routine appointment, the doctor said my blood pressure was beginning to rise and asked how active I was. On her recommendation, I started taking a brisk walk during my lunch break rather than staying at my computer. At first it seemed like an interruption, but now I return to work with more energy as well.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: When I'm writing essays, I used to keep every notification switched on in case friends were arranging something. Of course, every message led to a conversation, and an hour would disappear. I've started putting my phone in another room while I study because, without constant interruptions, I can actually focus on what I'm reading and finish much sooner. I catch up with friends afterwards, so I don't feel I'm missing out.

options.text: to have more time with family
to reduce their effect on the environment

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker leaves her phone downstairs because it helps her sleep better (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker takes lunch from home to reduce everyday spending (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker prepares the night before so she is no longer late for lectures (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker began walking at lunchtime after receiving advice from a doctor (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker keeps the phone away while studying in order to concentrate more effectively (evidence [23]).
